title: Scherenschnitte repeating border
subtitle: Folding and cutting a paper border in the Swiss-German scissor-cut tradition
excerpt: Scherenschnitte, scissor cuts, is a Swiss and German folk-art tradition of cutting intricate designs from folded paper. A repeating border is the most accessible starting point: a strip of paper folded accordion-style produces a design that repeats symmetrically when unfolded. The only tools needed are a sharp scalpel and a cutting mat; the skill is in drawing the design to account for the fold.
sourceNotes: Scherenschnitte (scissor-cutting) is a Swiss and German folk tradition documented from the seventeenth century. The technique is described in folk-art publications and museum collections across Switzerland and southern Germany. Standard designs appear in pre-1928 folk-art reference books and pattern collections.

glossaryTerms.definition: Literally 'scissor cuts' in German. A paper-cutting art form originating in Switzerland and southern Germany in the seventeenth century. Designs are cut from folded paper; when unfolded they reveal symmetrical or repeating patterns of positive and negative space.
A fold that alternates mountain and valley folds at regular intervals, causing the paper to fan out like an accordion bellows. Used in scherenschnitte to create a repeating pattern from a single cut.

In papercutting, the positive space is the paper left intact (the silhouette); the negative space is what has been cut away. Good scherenschnitte designs balance both: areas of solid paper against cut-through voids that create light.

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Use a sharp scalpel blade; a blunt one shifts the layers and tears. Replace at the first sign of dragging.

content.text: border designs work on a single principle: any shape you cut through the folded paper will appear in mirror image when the paper is unfolded. An
multiplies this mirror effect across several repetitions.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, cut to 60 × 210 mm (A4 width, 60 mm tall)
items.substitutions: Black paper for a traditional silhouette; white for a lighter look

items.name: Scalpel (10A blade)
items.substitutions: A very sharp craft knife No. 11 blade

content.text: Fold the strip in half lengthways (to 30 × 210 mm), this will give a mirrored design on both halves of the height.

Now accordion-fold the folded strip into equal sections across its width. For a 210 mm strip, folding into 6 equal sections gives 35 mm per panel, this is the repeat unit. Crease each fold sharply with a bone folder.

Draw the design on the top panel only, in pencil. Keep the design simple for a first attempt: a tulip or leaf shape with a small hole cut near the top fold. The folds are the axes of symmetry, anything you draw within one panel will mirror across both fold lines.

Cut all interior holes and curves with the scalpel on the cutting mat. Work from small details inward; do not cut the outline until the interior cuts are complete, or the panel will become floppy and shift.
Cut the outline last. Leave at least 3 mm of paper at every fold connection, or the border will come apart when unfolded.
Unfold carefully, pressing the fold lines flat with a bone folder. The repeating

items.symptom: The border comes apart when unfolded
items.cause: Cuts went all the way to the fold line at some point, cutting through the connection.
items.fix: Always leave 3 mm of paper at every fold. Tape small tears from the back with washi tape, it is thin and nearly invisible from the front.

items.symptom: The cut edge is ragged rather than clean
items.cause: Blade is blunt; or the layers shifted during cutting.
items.fix: Change the blade. Hold the stack firmly against the cutting mat with the fingertip just behind the cut line, moving the finger ahead of the blade as you work.
